title: What State Is Your Nervous System In Right Now?
description: Discover whether your nervous system is in a ventral vagal (safe), sympathetic (activated), or dorsal vagal (shutdown) state — and what that means for your healing.

questions.text: When you think about your day ahead, your first feeling is:

options.label: Numb or disconnected — like it doesn't matter

questions.text: Your breathing right now feels:

options.label: Shallow or tight in the chest
Slow but heavy, like an effort
I'm not really aware of it

questions.text: When someone you trust reaches out to connect, you typically:

options.label: Feel warm and want to engage
Feel a flicker of warmth but also some tension
Feel irritated or want to be left alone
Feel like you can't really respond — too flat

questions.text: Your body's energy level right now is:

options.label: Buzzy, restless, or hard to settle

questions.text: When you make a mistake, your immediate inner response is:

options.label: Nothing — I feel strangely blank about it

questions.text: Your jaw and shoulders right now feel:

options.label: Heavy and dropped, like they're weighted

questions.text: When you're in a social situation, you generally feel:

options.label: On guard or scanning for problems
Like you're watching from behind glass

questions.text: Your sleep has been:

options.label: Excessive — sleeping too much but never rested

questions.text: When you encounter a new challenge, you tend to:

options.label: Feel your heart rate rise and start problem-solving frantically
Feel overwhelmed and want to avoid it
Feel nothing — like it doesn't register

questions.text: Your digestion and appetite have been:

options.label: Tight, crampy, or appetite is off

questions.text: When you look in the mirror, you feel:

options.label: Like you're looking at a stranger
Anxious or want to look away quickly

questions.text: Your sense of hope about the future is:

options.label: Mostly absent — hard to imagine things improving
Anxious about it — afraid to hope

ventral.label: Ventral Vagal: Safe and Social
ventral.headline: Your nervous system is in a regulated, connected state.
ventral.body: Right now, your nervous system is operating from what Stephen Porges calls the ventral vagal state. You have access to social engagement, curiosity, and genuine connection. This doesn't mean life is perfect — it means your body feels safe enough to be present. This is the state where healing actually happens, where learning sticks, and where relationships feel real. The work here is to notice this state, name it, and learn what conditions support it so you can return to it more easily.
practices: Savoring moments of connection
Gentle movement or walking in nature
Journaling about what feels good right now

sympathetic.label: Sympathetic: Activated and On Guard
sympathetic.headline: Your nervous system is mobilized — running on high alert.
sympathetic.body: Your results suggest your nervous system is currently in a sympathetic activation state. This is the fight-or-flight branch — your body is mobilized, scanning for threat, and burning energy to keep you safe. This isn't a character flaw or a sign something is wrong with you. It's your biology doing exactly what it was designed to do. The problem is when this state becomes chronic. Sustained sympathetic activation depletes your system, disrupts sleep, digestion, and connection, and keeps you locked in a cycle of reactivity. The path forward starts with helping your body learn that the threat has passed.
practices: Extended exhale breathing (4 in, 8 out)
Cold water on the face or wrists
Vigorous movement to discharge activation
TRE (Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises)

dorsal.label: Dorsal Vagal: Shutdown and Withdrawn
dorsal.headline: Your nervous system has moved into a protective shutdown state.
dorsal.body: Your results suggest your nervous system may be in a dorsal vagal state — what Peter Levine calls the freeze or collapse response. This is the oldest branch of the autonomic nervous system, and it activates when the threat feels too big to fight or flee. The result is a kind of protective numbness: low energy, disconnection, difficulty feeling emotions or motivation. This is not weakness. It's your body's last-resort protection. The path back is slow and gentle — not forcing activation, but carefully building safety signals that let your system know it's okay to come back online.
practices: Gentle rhythmic movement (rocking, swaying)
Warm baths or heat on the body
Orienting exercises — slowly looking around the room
Small, achievable tasks to rebuild a sense of agency

title: How Is Trauma Showing Up in Your Body?
description: Identify the physical patterns that may be trauma responses — chronic tension, pain, numbness, digestive issues, and more — and understand what your body is trying to tell you.

questions.text: Do you experience chronic tension or pain in your neck, shoulders, or back without a clear physical cause?
Do you have digestive issues (IBS, nausea, stomach tightness) that seem connected to stress or emotions?
Do you find it difficult to feel sensations in parts of your body — like areas feel numb or disconnected?

options.label: No, I feel my body fairly well
Often — especially in my chest or pelvis
I feel generally disconnected from my body

questions.text: Do you startle easily — jumping at sounds, sudden movements, or unexpected touch?

options.label: My startle response feels out of control

questions.text: Do you hold your breath or breathe shallowly without realizing it?

options.label: Often — I catch myself holding my breath

questions.text: Do you experience unexplained fatigue that doesn't improve with rest?

options.label: It's a defining feature of my daily life

questions.text: Do you feel physically unsafe in your own body — like your body is the threat rather than the refuge?

options.label: No, my body feels like home
Sometimes I feel uncomfortable in my body
Often — I'd rather not be in my body
My body feels like enemy territory

questions.text: Do you have difficulty tolerating physical touch — even gentle, safe touch?

options.label: Often uncomfortable, even with people I trust

questions.text: Do you experience physical symptoms (headaches, skin issues, immune problems) that doctors can't fully explain?

options.label: Multiple unexplained symptoms are a constant

questions.text: When you're emotionally triggered, does your body respond before your mind catches up?

options.label: Rarely — I usually notice thoughts first
Often — my body reacts and I figure out why later
Always — my body is way ahead of my awareness

low.label: Minimal Somatic Impact
low.headline: Your body is carrying relatively few trauma signals right now.
low.body: Your responses suggest your body is not showing strong signs of stored trauma at this time. That's genuinely good news. It could mean you've done significant healing work, that your nervous system has good regulation capacity, or that you haven't experienced significant trauma. Whatever the reason, this is a strong foundation. The work now is maintenance and deepening — continuing to build body awareness and nervous system literacy so you can recognize early signals before they become chronic patterns.
practices: Regular body scan practice
Preventive somatic check-ins
Building body literacy through reading and practice

moderate.label: Moderate Somatic Impact
moderate.headline: Your body is holding some trauma patterns — and they're worth paying attention to.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest a moderate level of somatic trauma impact. You're experiencing some of the classic body-based signs: tension patterns, digestive sensitivity, startle responses, or disconnection from sensation. These aren't random. They're your body's intelligent adaptations to experiences that felt threatening. The good news is that at this level, somatic work tends to be very effective. Your nervous system still has significant capacity for regulation, and body-based practices can create real change relatively quickly.
practices: Somatic Experiencing or body-based therapy
Daily breathwork (extended exhale)
Grounding practices — especially feet-on-floor awareness
TRE exercises

high.label: Significant Somatic Impact
high.headline: Your body is carrying significant trauma — and it deserves real, skilled attention.
high.body: Your responses suggest your body is holding a significant amount of trauma. The patterns you're experiencing — chronic pain, disconnection, digestive issues, touch sensitivity, unexplained symptoms — are not character flaws or signs of weakness. They're the body's way of encoding experiences that were too much to process at the time. This level of somatic impact typically benefits most from working with a skilled somatic therapist who can help you approach this material safely, at the right pace. You don't have to do this alone, and you shouldn't have to.
practices: Find a somatic therapist (SE, Sensorimotor, or EMDR)
Gentle, trauma-sensitive yoga
Very slow, titrated body awareness practices
Build safety before processing

title: How Wide Is Your Window of Tolerance?
description: Assess your capacity to stay present with difficult emotions and sensations without going into overwhelm (hyperarousal) or shutdown (hypoarousal).

questions.text: When you feel a strong emotion, you can usually:

options.label: Stay with it and let it move through
Stay with it briefly before needing to distract
Feel it briefly before it overwhelms you
Either go numb or completely lose it

questions.text: When something stressful happens, you return to baseline:

options.label: It takes a day or more
I'm not sure I have a baseline to return to

questions.text: When someone raises their voice near you, you:

options.label: Feel activated but can manage it
Freeze, panic, or get very upset

questions.text: Your capacity to tolerate uncertainty is:

options.label: Generally okay — you can sit with not knowing
Intolerable — you need to know or you fall apart

questions.text: When you're in a conflict with someone you care about, you can usually:

options.label: Stay present and work through it
Get activated but come back to the conversation
Completely lose yourself in the reaction

questions.text: Your ability to be present in your body during everyday activities is:

options.label: Often difficult — you're frequently in your head
Very limited — you feel disconnected most of the time

questions.text: When you think about a past difficult experience, you:

options.label: Can reflect on it without being overwhelmed
Feel some activation but can stay present
Either go numb or feel like it's happening again

questions.text: Your capacity to experience joy, pleasure, or playfulness is:

wide.label: Wide Window of Tolerance
wide.headline: You have a well-developed capacity to stay present with difficult experiences.
wide.body: Your window of tolerance is wide — meaning you can experience a range of emotions and sensations without being overwhelmed or shutting down. This is a significant resource. It suggests either natural resilience, effective healing work, or both. A wide window doesn't mean you don't have hard experiences. It means your nervous system has the capacity to process them. The work here is to keep expanding this capacity and to use it in service of deeper healing.
practices: Deepen your practice with more challenging material
Support others — your regulation can co-regulate those around you
Explore more advanced somatic practices

moderate.label: Moderate Window of Tolerance
moderate.headline: Your window is workable — with some edges that need attention.
moderate.body: Your window of tolerance is moderate. You have real capacity to stay present, but there are edges where you tip into overwhelm or shutdown. This is very common, and it's workable. The goal isn't to never get activated — it's to expand the range of what you can tolerate while staying present. Somatic practices, breathwork, and titrated trauma work can all help widen this window over time.
practices: Daily grounding and regulation practice
Pendulation — moving between activation and resource
Breathwork for regulation
Somatic therapy to work the edges safely

narrow.label: Narrow Window of Tolerance
narrow.headline: Your window is currently narrow — and that's important information, not a verdict.
narrow.body: Your window of tolerance appears narrow right now. This means your nervous system is frequently tipping into either overwhelm (hyperarousal) or shutdown (hypoarousal), with limited time in the regulated middle zone. This is not a permanent state. Windows of tolerance can be widened with the right support and the right pacing. The key word is pacing — trying to push through too fast will narrow the window further. The work starts with building safety and resources, not with processing trauma.
practices: Start with resourcing — not trauma processing
Find a skilled somatic therapist
Very gentle grounding practices daily
Reduce unnecessary stressors where possible

title: Is People-Pleasing a Trauma Response for You?
description: Explore whether the fawn response — chronic people-pleasing, difficulty with boundaries, and self-abandonment — is operating as a trauma survival strategy in your life.

questions.text: When someone seems upset or displeased with you, your first instinct is to:

options.label: Check in with yourself first, then respond
Want to fix it but can usually hold your ground
Immediately try to smooth things over
Panic and do whatever it takes to restore approval

questions.text: Saying 'no' to someone you care about feels:

options.label: Normal — you can do it without much distress
Very difficult — you often say yes when you mean no
Nearly impossible — the anxiety is too intense

questions.text: In conflict situations, you tend to:

options.label: Stay present and work toward resolution
Get anxious but can hold your position
Immediately take the blame to end the conflict

questions.text: How often do you find yourself agreeing with people even when you don't actually agree?

options.label: Almost always — I don't know what I actually think

questions.text: When someone is angry — even if not at you — you feel:

options.label: Compelled to intervene and calm things down
Terrified and immediately try to appease

questions.text: Your sense of your own needs and preferences is:

options.label: Generally clear but sometimes hard to prioritize
Often unclear — you're not sure what you want
Very unclear — other people's needs always come first

questions.text: After spending time with others, you often feel:

options.label: Drained — like you gave too much
Empty and resentful — you performed the whole time

questions.text: When you receive criticism, even constructive feedback, you:

options.label: Can hear it and consider it
Feel deeply ashamed and want to fix it immediately

questions.text: Do you find yourself monitoring other people's moods and adjusting your behavior accordingly?

options.label: Occasionally, as a normal social skill
Often — you're always reading the room

questions.text: The idea of someone being disappointed in you feels:

options.label: Very distressing — you work hard to avoid it
Intolerable — like a threat to your safety

low.label: Minimal Fawn Patterns
low.headline: People-pleasing doesn't appear to be a dominant pattern for you.
low.body: Your responses suggest you have a relatively healthy relationship with your own needs and boundaries. You can generally say no, hold your position in conflict, and tolerate others' disappointment without it destabilizing you. This is a real resource. It doesn't mean you never accommodate others — it means you do so from choice rather than fear.
practices: Continue building self-awareness
Notice any situations where fawn patterns do emerge
Celebrate your capacity for healthy boundaries

moderate.label: Moderate Fawn Patterns
moderate.headline: People-pleasing is present — and it's worth exploring where it comes from.
moderate.body: Your responses suggest moderate fawn patterns. You have some capacity to hold your own needs, but there are situations where the pull to appease, agree, or smooth things over overrides your own truth. This is very common, especially for people who grew up in environments where expressing needs felt dangerous. The fawn response was smart once. The work is learning that you don't need it anymore — that your safety doesn't depend on other people's approval.
practices: Practice small acts of boundary-setting
Notice the physical sensation of fawn activation
Explore the early origins of this pattern
Work with a therapist on parts work or IFS

high.label: Strong Fawn Patterns
high.headline: People-pleasing appears to be a central organizing strategy for you — and it's costing you.
high.body: Your responses suggest strong fawn patterns. The fawn response is running your show — monitoring others' moods, suppressing your own needs, agreeing when you don't agree, and treating others' approval as a survival requirement. This is exhausting. And it makes sense. Somewhere along the way, your nervous system learned that keeping others happy was the safest strategy. That was probably true then. It's not true now. But your body doesn't know that yet. This is exactly the kind of pattern that somatic and parts-based work can address.
practices: Find a trauma-informed therapist (IFS, somatic, or EMDR)
Start noticing — not changing — the fawn activation first
Practice the pause: one breath before responding to requests
Read about fawn response and attachment trauma

title: Are You Ready to Start Somatic Healing Work?
description: Assess your current readiness for body-based trauma work — including your stability, resources, and what kind of support would serve you best right now.

questions.text: Your current life circumstances are:

options.label: Relatively stable — no major active crises

questions.text: You have at least one person in your life you can genuinely rely on for support:
Your relationship with your body right now is:

options.label: Uncomfortable — you'd rather not focus on it

questions.text: You have some capacity to tolerate uncomfortable sensations without immediately needing to escape them:

options.label: No — any discomfort triggers panic or shutdown

questions.text: You have access to at least one thing that reliably helps you feel calmer or safer:

options.label: Uncertain — things that used to help don't always work

questions.text: Your motivation for doing somatic work is:

options.label: Genuine curiosity and desire to heal
Mostly external pressure (someone else thinks I should)
I'm not sure I want to do this

questions.text: You can generally function in daily life — work, relationships, basic self-care:

options.label: Struggling significantly in one or more areas
Basic functioning is very difficult right now

questions.text: You have some understanding of what somatic therapy involves:

options.label: Yes, I've researched it and understand the basics
Very little — I'm mostly going on instinct
No idea — I just know I need help

questions.text: You're willing to go slowly — to prioritize safety over speed in your healing:

options.label: Yes, I understand why that matters
It's hard — I want to be done with this
No — I need this to be over fast

questions.text: You have some financial or practical access to therapeutic support:

options.label: Very limited — I'd need low-cost options

ready.label: Ready to Begin
ready.headline: You have a strong foundation for somatic healing work.
ready.body: Your responses suggest you're in a good position to begin or deepen somatic healing work. You have stability, some resources, motivation, and a willingness to go slowly. These are the real prerequisites — not having everything figured out, but having enough ground under your feet to do the work safely. The next step is finding the right practitioner and approach for your specific needs.
practices: Research somatic therapists in your area
Start with a consultation to find the right fit
Begin building daily somatic practices now
Read foundational books on somatic healing

building.label: Building Readiness
building.headline: You're on your way — with some foundations still to build.
building.body: Your responses suggest you have some readiness for somatic work, but there are areas where more foundation-building would help. This isn't a reason to wait indefinitely — it's a reason to be thoughtful about where you start. Self-directed practices, psychoeducation, and building your support network are all valuable first steps. A skilled somatic therapist can also help you build the foundations you need as part of the work itself.
practices: Focus on stabilization and resourcing first
Build your support network
Start with gentle self-directed somatic practices
Consider a therapist who specializes in stabilization

stabilize.label: Stabilize First
stabilize.headline: Right now, stabilization is more important than trauma processing.
stabilize.body: Your responses suggest that before diving into trauma processing work, the priority is stabilization. This means building safety, resources, and daily functioning. This is not a failure — it's wisdom. Trying to process trauma when your foundation is unstable often makes things worse, not better. The most important thing you can do right now is get support for your current level of distress, build one or two reliable calming strategies, and reduce unnecessary stressors where possible.
practices: Seek crisis support if needed
Focus on basic safety and daily functioning
Build one reliable calming practice
Consider a therapist focused on stabilization, not processing
